coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots surface chemistry drives fluorescent properties: New tools to distinguish isobaric peptides
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Distefano, Alessia
Calì, Federico
Gaeta, Massimiliano
Tuccitto, Nunzio
Auditore, Alessandro
Licciardello, Antonino
D'Urso, Alessandro
Lee, Kwang-Jin
Monasson, Olivier
Peroni, Elisa
Grasso, Giuseppe
coredata.dc:description: The possibility to design rational carbon dots surface functionalization for specific analytical and bioanalytical applications is hindered by the lack of a full knowledge of the surface chemical features driving fluorescent properties. In this model study, we have synthesized four different peptides, three of which are isobaric and not distinguishable by common MSMS experiments. After having characterized the peptides conformations by CD analyses, we have covalently bonded all four peptides to carbon dots by using different experimental procedures, which produce different functional groups on the carbon dots surface. The peptide orientations obtained on the differently functionalized surface of the nanoparticles were different and produced different fluorescent responses. The reported results indicate the possibility to design amino and carboxyl enriched surface carbon dots to answer specific chemical requirements, paving the way for the use of these nanoparticles as a versatile and useful new chemical and biochemical tool.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Peptide orientation
Surface chemistry
Covalent bond
Spectroscopy
Mass spectrometry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Solid phase peptide synthesis and RP-HPLC purification
Carbon Dots-peptides conjugation and fluorescence method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Amino-acidic sequences of the peptides considered in this work, Car is highlighted in red. In TAT1-Car, Car-TAT1 and T-Car-T, the Car molecule is bound to C-terminus, N-terminus and in the middle of TAT1 amino-acidic sequence, respectively.

Circular dichroism spectra for TAT1 peptide and its Car-derivatives in aqueous solution at room temperature. The peptide concentration is 5 µM and the pH = 4.5. In the inset, the CD spectra’s arithmetic differences are reported: (TAT1) - (T-Car-T), black dotted plot; (Car-TAT1) - (T-Car-T), red solid plot.
Fluorescence variation maps of activated CD–N after reaction with different polypeptide sequences, namely (a) TAT1-Car, (b) TAT1, (c) Car-TAT1, and (d) T-Car-T.
Fluorescence variation maps of activated CD–N after reaction with TAT1-Car, at different polypeptide concentrations, namely (a) 0.2, (b) 0.4, (c) 0.8, and (d) 1.4 mg/ml.

(a) Fluorescence variations of activated CD–N after reaction with TAT1-Car, at different peptide concentrations, as labelled in picture, under excitation wavelength of 370 nm; (b) ΔI trend as the concentration of TAT1-Car changes under emission wavelength of 450 nm.
Fluorescence variation maps of activated CD–COOH after reaction with different polypeptide sequences, namely (a) TAT1-Car, (b) TAT1, (c) Car-TAT1, and (d) T-Car-T.
Fluorescence variation maps of activated CD–COOH after reaction with TAT1-Car, at different polypeptide concentrations, namely (a) 0.2, (b) 0.3, (c) 0.4, and (d) 0.8 mg/ml.

(a) Fluorescence variations of activated citric CD after reaction with TAT1-Car, at different peptide concentrations, as labelled in picture, under excitation wavelength of 370 nm; (b) ΔI trend as the concentration of TAT1-Car changes under emission wavelength of 450 nm.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots surface chemistry drives fluorescent properties: New tools to distinguish isobaric peptides

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Sciences Department, University of Catania, Viale Andrea Doria 6, 95125 Catania, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical Sciences Department, University of Catania, Viale Andrea Doria 6, 95125 Catania, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CY Cergy Paris Université, CNRS, BioCIS, 95000 Cergy Pontoise, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CY Cergy Paris Université, CNRS, BioCIS, 95000 Cergy Pontoise, France

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Université Paris-Saclay, CNRS, BioCIS, 92290 Châtenay-Malabry, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Université Paris-Saclay, CNRS, BioCIS, 92290 Châtenay-Malabry, France

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CY Cergy Paris Université, CY PeptLab, 95000 Cergy Pontoise, France

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CY Cergy Paris Université, CY PeptLab

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CY Cergy Paris Université, CY PeptLab, 95000 Cergy Pontoise, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The possibility to design rational carbon dots surface functionalization for specific analytical and bioanalytical applications is hindered by the lack of a full knowledge of the surface chemical features driving fluorescent properties.

In this model study, we have synthesized four different peptides, three of which are isobaric and not distinguishable by common MSMS experiments. After having characterized the peptides conformations by CD analyses, we have covalently bonded all four peptides to carbon dots by using different experimental procedures, which produce different functional groups on the carbon dots surface. The peptide orientations obtained on the differently functionalized surface of the nanoparticles were different and produced different fluorescent responses. The reported results indicate the possibility to design amino and carboxyl enriched surface carbon dots to answer specific chemical requirements, paving the way for the use of these nanoparticles as a versatile and useful new chemical and biochemical tool.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots, owing to reduced toxicity and high solubility in water along with low costs and facile synthesis, have recently emerged as intriguing and sustainable nanoemitters with useful fluorescent properties [1], which can be used for several applications, such as intrabody molecular communication [2–4], metal sensing [5], bioimaging [6–8], diagnostics and theranostics [9–11]. In fact, functionalizing the carbon dots surface according to the desired application [12] is a straightforward strategy widely adopted for glucose [13], phosphate [14], pH [15], metal ions [16,17] and amino-acids [18] detection. In addition, very recently carbon dots have been used for anti-inflammation therapies [19], drug delivery [20], anti-bacterial activity [21] and as photo-thermal agents [22]. Appropriate chiral precursors can be employed to build chiral carbon-based quantum dots, which, in turn, were reported to be able to imprint the chiral information to self-assembled systems so as to achieve tuneable chiroptical supramolecular devices [23,24]. For these reasons, recent attention by the scientific community has been focused on carbon dots synthetic methods, size control, modification strategies and photoelectric properties [25,26]. There are many approaches for functionalizing the surface of carbon dots, ranging from covalent and π–π interactions [27] to sol–gel technology [28]. Usually, highly specific carbon dots preparation and functionalization are needed in order to achieve the desired use, as even the chirality of the nanoparticles can have a massive impact on their interaction with biomolecules and supramolecular systems [29]. However, although peptides [30,31] and proteins [32,33] have been attached to carbon dots in order to achieve specific analytical properties, the molecular features responsible for the specific carbon dots fluorescent responses have not been always fully elucidated [34,35]. In this scenario, to the best of our knowledge, an investigation of the effect that different surface orientations of specific peptides have on the fluorescent properties of these nanoparticles is still missing in the current literature. For this reason, we have aimed at filling this gap by designing a carbon dots-peptides model study to unveil some of the chemical features affecting the fluorescent responses detected in carbon dots-peptides systems. In this manner, the design of the most appropriate carbon dots chemistry for specific uses in peptides detection becomes feasible. Particularly, we have synthesized four different peptides having high similarity in the amino-acidic sequences, three of them being also isobaric. The capability to distinguish between isobaric chemical species has always been a challenging analytical task [36,37], which is normally tackled by complex mass spectrometric approaches [38]. Here, we report on the correlation between peptides orientation on the carbon dots surface and their fluorescent properties. The comprehension of the surface molecular features driving the fluorescent response enables the design of a tailored covalent attachment of the peptides to the carbon dots surface which allows us to distinguish among isobaric peptides by a simple fluorescent based method. Our results pave the way to the rational design of carbon dots surface functionalization for useful specific analytical and bioanalytical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solid phase peptide synthesis and RP-HPLC purification
ce:section.ce:para: Peptides were purified by semi-preparative RP-HPLC using a fully automated Waters Preparative System equipped with Phenomenex Synergi 4u Fusion-RP C18 (150 × 10 mm) column operated at 4 mL/min at 25 °C. The solvent systems used were: A (0.1% TFA in H2O) and B (0.1 % TFA in CH3CN). The purity of the peptides was analyzed by analytical RP-UPLC ESI-MS (Waters Acquity UPLC coupled to a Waters 3100 ESI-SQD MS) supplied with a Luna Omega PS C18 (1.6 μm 2.1 × 50 mm) column at 35 °C at 0.6 mL/min with solvent systems A (0.1% TFA in H2O) and B (0.1% TFA in CH3CN). Data were acquired and processed using MassLynx software (Waters, Milford, MA, USA). The peptides primary sequences are reported in detail in Fig. 1. All the peptides were obtained with a purity ≥90%.

All samples, stock and work solutions were prepared by using ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm, TOC 1 pbm) obtained from PURELAB Flex 3 system by Elga Veolia company at room conditions. In detail, all peptides stock solutions were prepared by dissolving the proper amount of the solid in ultrapure water to reach a final concentration of 0.25 mM. The as-prepared aqueous solution exhibited pH values around 3.5. Then, the work solutions of each peptide (5 µM, total volume = 2.5 mL) were obtained from the corresponding stock solution by diluting in ultrapure water. After the dilution, the pH increased to about 5.0–5.5. Since the peptide-carbon dots systems were investigated at pH = 4.5, we adjusted the pH value of each work solution to 4.5 through small aliquots (i.e., not more than 0.5 µL) of HCl 0.1 M (see Fig. 1S of the Supplementary Information).

It is worth mentioning that no differences in both circular dichroism (CD) and UV/Vis spectra were detected by changing the pH values (from 5.5 to 4.5) in all peptide solutions. Indeed, due to their high theoretical isoelectric point, pI theo , (TAT1, pI theo = 8.97; TAT1-Car, pI theo = 8.65; Car-TAT1, pI theo = 8.65; T-Car-T pI theo = 8.65), we did not expect remarkable changes in terms of net-positive charges and, as a consequence, in the final conformational arrangement (secondary structure).

A JASCO V-530 UV/Vis spectrophotometer equipped with a 1 cm path-length cell was used for the spectroscopic investigations at room temperature. A quartz cuvette (path-length = 1 cm, Hellma company) was used for all spectroscopic measurements.

Mass spectrometric analyses were performed on a Thermo Scientific LCQ-DECA ion trap mass spectrometer equipped with an ESI ion source operating in MS and MS/MS positive ion mode under the following conditions: capillary temperature 220 °C, sheath gas 10 a.u.; source voltage 3.5 kV and capillary voltage 18 V. Repetitive mass spectra were acquired in positive ion mode in the m/z range 100–2000. MS/MS analysis was performed as follows: (i) full-scan MS in the m/z range 100–2000; (ii) Isolation width: 2; (iii) MS/MS analysis of with normalized collision energy: 17–27 a.u. and activation Q: 0.250. Mass calibration was made using a standard mixture of caffeine (Mr. 194.1 Da), MRFA peptide (Mr. 524.6 Da) and Ultramark (Mr. 1621 Da). Data acquisition and data analyses were performed using the Xcalibur v. 3.0 Software. Results obtained are reported in ESI, Fig. 2 S, 3S and 4S.

Amine-rich carbon dots (CD–N) were prepared by thermal decomposition of citric acid and urea (both from Merck, Italy). Briefly, 5 g of citric acid and 5 g of urea were weighed and placed on a hot plate at approximately 220 °C. The pyrolysis process lasts for 10–15 min, until a kind of yellowish caramel is obtained. This is cooled to room temperature and then 30 mL of ultrapure water are added to it. The result is a suspension of CD–N with other by-products of thermal decomposition. In order to obtain a suspension as clean as possible, a dialysis process is continued using a membrane with a cut-off of 14 kDa (Merck, Italy). Dialysis ends when the dialysis water no longer fluoresces, i.e. after about two days. The resulting suspension is then subjected to cryo-centrifugation at 3 °C, at 6000 rpm for one hour. The supernatant corresponds to the final suspension, one desired for subsequent characterization and experiments. Carboxyl enriched carbon dots (CD–COOH) were prepared by thermal decomposition of citric acid alone. 5 g of citric acid were weighed and placed on a hot plate at approximately 220 °C. Exactly the same procedure was carried out as for the preparation of CD–N. The only difference is when, after the decomposition product has cooled down, a 0.2 M NaOH solution is added to obtain a suspension with a neutral pH (6–7). With this thermal decomposition method, a typical CD–N or CD–COOH concentration of about 2 mg/ml is obtained. Scanning probe microscopy analyses confirmed the nanometric size of the synthesised particles (data not shown), whereas FT-IR analysis demonstrated the presence of different functional groups on the surfaces of the two differently synthesized carbon dots (see Supplementary Information, Fig. 5S). The FT-IR spectra of the two differently synthesized carbon dots are reported in Fig. 5S. In the case of CD–COOH, there are the characteristic absorption peaks for the stretching vibration of CO at 1710 cm−1, CO bonds at 1204 cm−1, OH at 3360 and 3030 cm−1, and CH or OH bending at 1392 cm−1. Fig. 5Sb reports the FT-IR spectrum of CD–N, which is characterized by a different fingerprint zone between 1000 and 2000 cm−1, including symmetric stretching vibration at 1568 cm−1 for a NH bond and several characteristic peaks at 2942 and 2854 cm−1 related to CH2 asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations.

In addition, the elemental analysis, performed by PerkinElmer 2400 Series II CHNS/O on freeze-dried samples, confirmed the presence of nitrogen in the CD–N. Elemental composition of CD–COOH and CD–N were, respectively, C 64(±2)%; O 33(±2)%; H 3(±2)%, and C 68(±2)%; O 18(±2)%; N 14(±2)%; H 3(±2)%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon Dots-peptides conjugation and fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: CD–N/CD–COOH activation was carried out by means of a solution of EDC (1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide hydrochloride, from Merck, Italy) and a solution of NHS (N-hydroxy succinimide, from Merck, Italy), with a molar ratio of 2/1 (EDC/NHS). Both solutions were added to CD–N/CD–COOH suspensions in which the addition of EDC is followed by NHS 1–2 min later. Once the activation of CD–N/CD–COOH surface was reached (10 min later), peptides were added to CD–N/CD–COOH at the indicated concentration.

After the peptides addition, all resulting suspensions have the same pH value of 4.5. All the fluorescence measurements were performed using a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence variation is given by the difference between the activated CD–N/CD–COOH subsequently bound to the polypeptide chain and the purely activated CD–N/CD–COOH. The CD–N/CD–COOH suspensions used in all experiment have the same concentration (0.9 mg/mL). The peptide concentration used in all cases was 0.4 mg/mL) Evidence that conjugation takes place between peptides and the surface of carbon dots are given by fluorescence variation maps (Figure 6S). Indeed, if CD–N are not activated by EDC/NHS and are made to interact with the Car-TAT1 chain, as it can be seen from Figure 6S, there are no obvious differences in the fluorescence spectra CD–N as such and CD–N together with Car-TAT1. In this case, we obtain a mixture of carbon dots and peptides and not a conjugation between the two systems. This effect is clearly visible in the fluorescence variation map (Figure 6S, c) where only very slight variations within the experimental noise can be observed. On the other hand, as demonstrated below, if the CD–N surface is activated with EDC/NHS, a stronger interaction (conjugation) of the peptide with the nanoparticles is obtained, resulting in clear variations in fluorescence. Same consideration can be done in the case of CD–COOH.

The samples used for ToF-SIMS analysis were prepared using the drop casting method. A small drop of a 4 mg/ml solution of each SUBSTANCE were cast on a flat silicon surface. Prior to the sample deposition the silicon surface was cleaned by UV/O3 treatment. After the drop deposition, the solvent was allowed to evaporate under low vacuum conditions (being the NANOPARTICLES hygroscopic) leaving an ultrathin solid film.

ToF-SIMS measurements were performed in a ToF-SIMS IV instrument (ION-TOF GmbH), using a Bi3 2+ analysis beam, operating at beam energy of 50 keV and beam current 0.1 pA. Positive secondary ions were acquired for 300 s, from a primary ion beam rastered area of 300x300 µm2. Under these conditions, the primary ion fluence is about 2 × 1011 ions/cm2, assuring the so called “static conditions”.

A small peptide (TAT1) derived from the transcriptional activator TAR (TAT) protein has recently drawn some attention due to its capability to stimulate proteasome activity [39]. MS-MS experiments showed great stability for this peptide (see ESI), so we have chosen the TAT1 amino-acidic sequence as a base to construct the model peptides needed in this work. Indeed, we have synthesized the other three isobaric peptides TAT1-Car, Car-TAT1 and T-Car-T, simply adding carnosine (Car) to the C- and N-terminus as well as in the middle of the peptide sequence, respectively (see Fig. 1). The addition of the Car moiety to the TAT-1 sequence was dictated by the interest that our group has towards Car [40,41]. Due to the peptides high stability, the MS-MS experiments (see ESI, Fig. 2S and 3S) are not able to clearly distinguish the investigated isobaric peptides. Particularly, Car-TAT1 and T-Car-T produced exactly the same fragmentation pattern (Fig. 3 S), showing high stability and a MSMS spectrum with an unexpected low number of peptide fragments. In addition, owing to a multitude of non-covalent interactions within the peptide’s chain [42,43], overall, the primary sequence can affect the final peptides conformation in aqueous media [44,45]. Consequently, the resulting conformational structure may entail different and particular outcomes throughout the interaction with CD–N/CD–COOH.

For this reason, firstly, we have investigated the conformations of such peptides alone in water by using spectroscopic methods, that is, CD and UV/Vis measurements. Indeed, changes in peptides secondary structure can be easily revealed by the CD technique through the selective absorption of the left and right circularly polarized light [46–49]. Our spectroscopic investigations are reported in Fig. 2 and Fig. 1S (see ESI). Apparently, by the analysis of the CD spectra, all the peptides tested seem arranged in random coil conformation. Indeed, the presence of a negative CD signal just below 200 nm, a positive band at about 218 nm and a very weak negative band at 235 nm are the characteristic CD signals for the random coil conformation. However, since the concentration of the analyzed peptides is the same for all experiments, the observed differences in the CD signal intensity suggest that some weak conformational changes, adopted by the peptides, occur.

The dichroic spectrum of the T-Car-T peptide is perfectly in line with random coil conformation (Fig. 2, blue solid plot). Even TAT1 peptide mainly displays a random coil conformation (Fig. 2, black dotted plot). However, as a result of the arithmetic difference between the CD spectra of TAT1 with T-Car-T, a mixed CD signal of β-sheet and β-turn conformations, characterized by a negative band at about 216 nm, a positive one near 195 nm (β-sheet), a negative band near 225 nm and a strong positive transition between 200 nm and 205 nm (β-turn), appear (see inset of Fig. 2, black dotted plot). This result indicates that TAT1 is partially arranged in β-conformations, which is completely lost when the Car sequence is inserted in the middle of the TAT1 peptide.

Similar behaviour is noted when the Car is bound to the N-terminus, that is in the case of Car-TAT1 (Fig. 2, red solid plot). Indeed, the CD spectrum of the Car-TAT1 peptide indicates that this peptide mainly adopts the random coil conformation, however, a short portion of β-sheet conformation seems to be present. Also in this case, the presence of the β-sheet conformation is confirmed by the arithmetic difference between the CD spectra of Car-TAT1 and T-Car-T (see inset of Fig. 2, red solid plot). On the contrary, the CD spectra of TAT1-Car (see Fig. 2, black solid plot) displays negligible differences if compared to the CD spectrum of T-Car-T, indicating that TAT1-Car mainly adopts a random coil conformation.

Therefore, CD results indicate that the Car residue and its position along the TAT-1 chain affect the final peptide conformation in water. Generally, owing to numerous CH, NH, CO bonds and their high-energy electronic transitions, the peptides UV/Vis spectra show a continuum-absorbance in the far UV range. Accordingly, the UV/Vis spectra reported in Fig. 1
S (see ESI) show similar shapes, so no further information can be extrapolated by UV/Vis spectra.

Once the structures and the conformations of the four different peptides have been assessed, we have investigated how these peptides could affect the fluorescent properties of carbon dots. In this perspective, it is important to highlight that the simple mixing of any of these peptides with carbon dots does not produce any detectable change in their fluorescent properties (data not shown). For this reason, we have decided to covalently attach the various peptides to the carbon dots surface. Unfortunately, the intense absorbance of carbon dots in the same absorbance range of peptides hinders to record the CD spectra of carbon dots after conjugations with peptides. However, we have confirmed the covalent attachment of the peptides on the carbon dots surface by TOF-SIMS analysis. Fig. 7
S and 8S (see ESI) show the comparison of some relevant portions of the ToF-SIMS spectra acquired in the case of CD–COOH and CD–N compared with spectra acquired from samples prepared by adding Car-TAT1 in the presence or absence of EDC/NHS activator. The results clearly show that characteristic peaks of amino acid fragments can only be identified on the surfaces of carbon dots which have been previously activated by EDC/NHS.

Once the covalent attachment of the peptides was demonstrated, we tested the peptides-functionalized CD–N fluorescent features, in order to develop a CD–N experimental method which could allow discerning between isobaric peptides (TAT1-Car, Car-TAT1 and T-Car-T). Fig. 3 shows, from a to d, the fluorescence variation maps of the nanoparticles bearing TAT1-Car, TAT1, Car-TAT1, and T-Car-T, respectively. The fluorescence variation is given by the difference between the activated CD–N subsequently bound to the polypeptide chain and the purely activated CD–N. The CD–N suspensions used have the same concentration (0.9 mg/mL). Maps (c) and (d) have the same positive and negative variation zones, but their respective intensities change; maps (a) and (b) give significant variations in comparison to the other two but also between themselves. It is undoubtedly evident that variations in the fluorescence of CD–N are dependent on the type of peptide bound on their surface, allowing us to discriminate between isobaric peptides. In addition, Fig. 4 shows four fluorescence variation maps obtained at the four different concentrations indicated of the peptide TAT1-Car. As the polypeptide concentration increases, the fluorescence of the CD–N decreases, resulting in greater negative variations clearly visible in the maps. This effect is due to the increase in surface coverage of the nanoparticles as the concentration of the peptide chain increases. To confirm this trend, we investigated how the fluorescence changes at a specific excitation wavelength, i.e. 370 nm, indicated by the white dotted line in Fig. 4d. Fig. 5a shows the fluorescence intensity variation when the CD–N suspensions are irradiated at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. At lower concentrations of peptide chains, such as 0.2 and 0.4 mg/mL, there is little decrease in the fluorescence of the carbon dots and for some emission wavelength values there is a positive variation. On the other hand, for larger peptide chain concentrations, such as 0.8 and 1.4 mg/mL, we can confirm a markedly negative change in fluorescence. Fig. 5b demonstrates this by restoring the ΔI under an emission wavelength of 450 nm, which is the typical maximum fluorescence value for carbon dots. Results reported in Figs. 4 and 5 can be interpreted by considering that, at low peptide concentrations, there are many –NH2 groups on the CD–N surface which are not yet linked to the –COOH terminal groups of the peptide molecules (low peptide coverage on the surface), so that higher peptides concentrations increase surface coverage and change fluorescent CD–N response.

A completely different situation is indeed observed when CD have only –COOH groups on their surface (CD–COOH) and the peptides can be linked by their terminal as well as side chains –NH2 groups. In Fig. 6, from (a) to (d), the fluorescence variation maps of the nanoparticles with TAT1-Car, TAT1, Car-TAT1, and T-Car-T, respectively, are reported. In this case, the attachment of the peptide to the surface can occur in many different ways, due to the presence of several reacting amino groups in the peptide sequences, so that multiple linkages within the same peptide molecule can also occur. This disordered peptide coverage of the CD–COOH surface causes a strong similarity of the average chemical environment for the three isobaric peptides, giving rise to very similar fluorescent responses. The only peptide inducing a quite different fluorescent variation map is TAT1, as it has a shorter peptide sequence. In this case, the absence of Car in the peptide sequence causes a Δfluo map that differs from the others, in which the absence of the negative variation zone centred at about 250 nm (λ exc) and 500 nm (λ emi), which appears in the other three maps, is easily identifiable (Fig. 6b). Finally, Fig. 7 shows four fluorescence variation maps obtained for the four different indicated concentrations of the peptide TAT1-Car. Unlike the maps reported in Fig. 5, this time the same fluorescence changes are obtained even though the concentration of the peptide varies. However, the effect obtained is the same as that seen previously, i.e. the addition of the peptide chains causes a negative change in fluorescence that is strongly visible in the maps shown. The latter observation is even more evident in this second case as no areas of positive fluorescence variation are observed in any of the four maps analysed.

In summary, we found that the variation in fluorescence of CD–COOH, differently from the case of CD–N, is independent from the concentration of the peptide chain considered. Confirmation of this is evident in Fig. 8 , especially in graph (b), where a completely flat trend of fluorescence variation (ΔI) is obtained as the concentration of the TAT1-Car chain changes at 450 nm in emission. This result is easily explained by considering that the saturation of the CD–COOH surface by TAT1-Car is already reached at very low peptide concentrations, due to the multiple binding sites available in the peptide chain (terminal and side chains –NH2 groups) in this case.

In this work, the spectrometric analyses revealed a great stability for the peptides under investigation, revealing the impossibility to distinguish between Car-TAT1 and T-Car-T by mass spectrometry. In addition, the spectroscopic behaviour of the reported peptides was also scrutinized, unveiling some conformational differences ascribable to the position of Car along the TAT-1 peptide chain. In particular, although all peptides show a preferential random coil secondary structure under acid pH values, in our experimental conditions, the TAT1 peptide appears to be partially arranged in β-conformations, whereas the latter are completely lost when the Car is placed in the middle of the TAT1 peptide. Analogously, when Car is linked to the TAT1 N-terminus, although the resulting Car-TAT1 peptide maintains a predominant random coil conformation, a partial arrangement in β-conformations cannot be excluded.

After this spectroscopic and spectrometric characterization, we have attached the peptides to the surface of two different functionalized carbon dots, named CD–N and CD–COOH. We have demonstrated that our chemical approach allows a covalent attachment of the peptides on the carbon dots surface (fluorescent and TOF-SIMS results) and only such carbon dots-peptides conjugation can alter the carbon dots fluorescent properties. Finally and more importantly, we have demonstrated that if the peptides are attached in a very ordinated manner through their –COOH terminal group as in the case of CD–N, the fluorescence variation maps allow us to distinguish different peptide concentrations as well as different isobaric peptides. On the contrary, using CD–COOH and attaching the peptides through their multiple –NH2 moieties present in the peptides chain, a much more randomized situation occurs and it becomes impossible to distinguish between different peptide concentrations as surface CD–COOH saturation is reached even at very low concentration values. Analogously, isobaric peptides become indistinguishable by CD–COOH, due to the randomness of the peptide anchoring sites.

The reported findings demonstrate the possibility to use carbon dots as a new tool to distinguish isobaric peptides, which are indistinguishable by mass spectrometry and pave the way for the rational design of carbon dots chemical features to be tailored according to the application purposes. Indeed, we have succeeded to control the orientation of the peptidic shell according to the carbon dots surface chemistry, in order to have functionalized carbon dots whose peculiar fluorescent properties could be used for sensitive detection of isobaric peptides.

Alessia Distefano: Methodology, Data curation. Federico Calì: Methodology. Massimiliano Gaeta: Data curation. Nunzio Tuccitto: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Alessandro Auditore: Data curation. Antonino Licciardello: Data curation, Formal Analysis. Alessandro D'Urso: Data Curation, Formal Analysis. Kwang-Jin Lee: Data curation. Olivier Monasson: Data curation. Elisa Peroni: Conceptualization, Supervision. Giuseppe Grasso: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by University of Catania Programma Ricerca di Ateneo Unict 2020‐2022‐Linea 2 (Project: 3N‐ORACLE) and by the CY advanced studies of the CY Cergy Paris University (AAP CYAS 2021). Alessia Distefano was supported by the PhD program in Chemical Sciences, University of Catania. Federico Calì was supported by the PhD program in Materials Science and Nanotechnology, University of Catania. The authors gratefully acknowledge prof. Vincenzo Cunsolo and prof. Rosaria Saletti of the Organic Mass Spectrometry Lab of the Department of Chemical Sciences of the University of Catania, who carried out the MS spectra and for the availability of the LTQ mass spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.06.050.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
